train_pairs.target: I came and saw the man and the dog.
You(sg) sing and I get up.
He saw the house and I took the pig.
She will get up and will take the bread.
We will go away and will sing.
They eat the bread and drink the water.

train_pairs.source: Anon ye weng wengambaranuuwa awon ye weng wengambaraneen.
train_pairs.target: We will hear the dog and he will hear the pig.
I drank the water and she saw the fish.
He takes the bread and eats it.

test_pairs.target: She took the bread and ate it.
She comes and we go away.
They will hear the dog and will get up.
I will see the fish and you will see theman.
He sees the water and I drink it.

steps.accumulated_rules:
- The language might use a Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) word order, given that "Meneni" (I) precedes "karuwa" (came) and "anona" (saw) in the first example, and "Ok" (I) precedes "anaana" (she) in the third example.
- Verbs might be marked for person, as suggested by the different forms for "I", "you", and "he" actions, and the different forms for "we" and "he" in "Wengambaranuuwa" and "Wengambaraneen".
- The language could use a system of affixes or particles to indicate grammatical relationships, though no clear pattern emerges from the given examples, but the suffix "-nuuwa" and "-neen" might indicate person agreement in "Wengambaranuuwa" and "Wengambaraneen".
- There appears to be a pattern of suffixation or prefixation to indicate future tense or person, as seen in "Yeediriin" (I get up) and "Yeederenu" (she will get up), and "Wingkeewa" (you sing) and "Wingkanuub" (will sing), and possibly in "Wengambaranuuwa" (we will hear) and "Wengambaraneen" (he will hear).
- The language may have a plural form for the first person, as indicated by "Wenenub" (we), and possibly a dual or plural form for other persons.
- The language might have a way to express coordination, as seen in "Oma oka aniib" (They eat the bread and drink the water) and "Ok anaana oon wedmeen" (I drank the water and she saw the fish), possibly using a simple juxtaposition of clauses.

steps.accumulated_rules:
- The language appears to use a Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) word order, given the consistent placement of the subject before the verb and the object in the provided examples.
- Verbs are likely marked for person, as suggested by the different forms for "I", "you", and "he" actions, and the different forms for "we" and "he" in "Wengambaranuuwa" and "Wengambaraneen".
- The language uses a system of affixes or particles to indicate grammatical relationships, with the suffix "-nuuwa" and "-neen" possibly indicating person agreement in "Wengambaranuuwa" and "Wengambaraneen", and the prefix "Yeeder-" possibly indicating future tense or person in "Yeediriin" and "Yeederenu".
- The language has a pattern of suffixation or prefixation to indicate future tense or person, as seen in "Yeediriin" (I get up) and "Yeederenu" (she will get up), and "Wingkeewa" (you sing) and "Wingkanuub" (will sing), and possibly in "Wengambaranuuwa" (we will hear) and "Wengambaraneen" (he will hear).
- The language has a plural form for the first person, as indicated by "Wenenub" (we), and possibly a dual or plural form for other persons.
- The language expresses coordination using a simple juxtaposition of clauses, as seen in "Oma oka aniib" (They eat the bread and drink the water) and "Ok anaana oon wedmeen" (I drank the water and she saw the fish), and in the new examples "Yeederenib miniib" (They get up and come), "Wingkenu wunuun" (She sings and goes away), and "Om bene aneen" (He takes the bread and eats it).
- The language may use a specific word order or particle to indicate coordination, as the new examples suggest a possible pattern of verb-subverb or action-consequence.
